coredata.dc:title: Efficient synthesis of green nitrogen-doped carbon dots as a versatile nanoprobe for antibacterial, cytotoxic, in-vitro imaging, and anti-counterfeit applications
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Kalifathullah, Samira Khathoon
Alaguvel, Sangeetha
Sundaramurthy, Devikala
coredata.dc:description: In this study, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were synthesized using Boerhavia diffusa leaves as a novel & natural carbon source. These N-CDs serve as fluorescent nanoprobes for imaging tumour cells and exhibit multifunctional properties, including non-toxicity, antibacterial effectiveness, and suitability for anti-counterfeiting applications. The structural, optical, and morphological characteristics of the N-CDs were confirmed through X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS), UV–visible spectroscopy, and Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM). These analyses verified the amorphous and graphitic carbon structure, uniform spherical distribution, and elemental composition, highlighting the surface functionalities of the N-CDs. They demonstrated strong antibacterial activity against E. coli and S. aureus. Additionally, MDA-MB-231 cells were clearly visible after 24 h at an N-CD concentration of 512 µg/mL, confirming non-toxicity. The N-CDs promise as fluorescent bioimaging probes for MDA-MB-231 cells and as anti-counterfeit agents.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-carbon dots
Anti-bacterial
Cytotoxicity
Bio-imaging
Security ink

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of N-CDs from Boerhavia diffusa leaves extract
Structural characteristics of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs)
TEM analysis and zeta potential of N-CDs
Cell cytotoxicity and bio-imaging of N-CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the synthesis process for N-CDs derived from Boerhavia diffusa leaves.
A) UV–visible spectroscopy of N-CDs (inlet: N-CDs under normal light and UV-light); b) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs at different excitation wavelengths.
A) XRD pattern and b) FT-IR spectrum (in ATR mode) of the N-CDs.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of N-CDs (inlet: pie-chart of the percentage of different elements present), scanning of (b) C 1s spectra (c) N 1s spectra, and (d) O 1s spectra of N-CDs.
TEM images of N-CDs (a–c) under different magnification; (d) SAED pattern; (e) EDX of N-CDs.
(a) Particle Size Distribution and (b) Zeta Potential of N-CDs.
(a–c) Zone of Inhibition of E. coli and S. aureus compared with Ciproflaxin as standard.
Cytotoxicity of N-CDs against MDA MB231 cell line.
Fluorescence images of MDA-MB231 cells with N-CDs: (a) under bright field and (b) under 400 nm UV light.
Photographic images of handwritten words using N-CDs under (a) normal light and (b) UV light.
Cell viability of MDA MB 231 cell line.

head.ce:title: Efficient synthesis of green nitrogen-doped carbon dots as a versatile nanoprobe for antibacterial, cytotoxic, in-vitro imaging, and anti-counterfeit applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Engineering and Technology, SRM Institute of Science and Technology, Kattankulathur, Tamil Nadu - 603203, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: SRM Institute of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: epartment of Chemistry, College of Engineering and Technology, SRM Institute of Science and Technology, Kattankulathur, Tamil Nadu - 603203, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were synthesized using Boerhavia diffusa leaves as a novel & natural carbon source. These N-CDs serve as fluorescent nanoprobes for imaging tumour cells and exhibit multifunctional properties, including non-toxicity, antibacterial effectiveness, and suitability for anti-counterfeiting applications. The structural, optical, and morphological characteristics of the N-CDs were confirmed through X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS), UV–visible spectroscopy, and Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM). These analyses verified the amorphous and graphitic carbon structure, uniform spherical distribution, and elemental composition, highlighting the surface functionalities of the N-CDs. They demonstrated strong antibacterial activity against E. coli and S. aureus. Additionally, MDA-MB-231 cells were clearly visible after 24 h at an N-CD concentration of 512 µg/mL, confirming non-toxicity. The N-CDs promise as fluorescent bioimaging probes for MDA-MB-231 cells and as anti-counterfeit agents.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Recent advancements in nanomaterials have given rise to carbon-based nanomaterials. Because of their remarkable qualities, nanomaterials in the carbon-based nanomaterial family have been extensively researched for a range of applications [1–5]. Amongst the list of carbon nanomaterials, our candidate the Carbon dots (CDs) have allured significant attention across diverse interdisciplinary research fields in recent decades [6,7]. They are considered as the smaller nanoparticles whose size is lesser than 10 nm and are spherical in shape. Due to this small size and structure, they possess very unique properties making them a promising candidate in the field of nanotechnology [8,9]. Their appeal stems from their straightforward and cost-effective synthesis, minute structures, bright and adjustable fluorescence, remarkable solubility in various solvents, excellent photostability, low toxicity, and exceptional biocompatibility [10–14]. Owing to their unique characteristics, they participate in various chemical and biomedical applications such as sensing [15], photocatalysis [16], drug delivery [17], bio-imaging, [18] and Gene therapy [19].

Compared to their chemically produced cousins, green carbon dots, or CDs, have attracted greater interest. Through a variety of synthesis techniques, biomass has recently become a well-liked and sustainable source for creating carbon dots [20–22]. Chemical oxidation, hydrothermal carbonization, microwave-assisted synthesis, solvothermal procedures, laser ablation, thermolysis, atmospheric plasma-based synthesis, electrochemical methods, and ultrasonic techniques are some of these techniques. The size and elemental makeup of carbon dots can be altered using a variety of synthesis methods, which influences the dots' characteristics [23]. For example, N.S. Karthikeyan and colleagues created carbon dots using black gram husk for use in catalytic dye reduction [24]. In a similar vein, Gwajeong et al. created carbon dots for luminous chemo-sensor applications using wasted coffee grounds [25]. Additionally, Atchudan et al. developed carbon dots from kiwi fruit peel for bioimaging purposes of both normal and cancerous cells, as well as for fluorescent ink applications [26].

Heteroatom-doped green CDs have been emerging in recent days. The common dopants used are nitrogen [27], sulphur [28], phosphorous [29], boron [30], etc. Amongst these, Nitrogen doping has been considered the most effective one. Due to the nitrogen atom's similar size to the carbon atom, its high electronegativity, and the presence of lone pairs with a strong affinity for the sp2 carbon domain, it exhibits favourable interactions. The presence of lone-pair electrons and the introduction of defect sites can create new active sites in CDs, enhancing their activity [31,32]. For both research and therapeutic applications, bioimaging is crucial because it makes it easier to observe and analyze biological processes at the molecular and subcellular levels as well as in tiny animal models [33]. Key requirements for effective bioimaging agents are minimal cytotoxicity, high fluorescence quantum yield, and superior biocompatibility. These conditions are met by nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs), which makes them attractive options for bioimaging applications [34,35]. Not only for bioimaging but it is suitable for other biological applications like cytotoxic studies, anti-bacterial, anti-cancer, and also in drug delivery systems. Tejwan et al., have studied the bio-imaging using CDs and its anti-oxidant and anti-bacterial activity using Rutin-loaded CDs [36]. Prakash et al., have studied the in vitro and in vivo toxicity of N-CDs and bio-imaging of bacterial and fungal growth [37].

In this study, we used a straightforward, environmentally friendly hydrothermal process to successfully create novel & extremely luminous nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) with a vivid green emission. Boerhavia diffusa leaf extract was used to create the N-CDs, and research has been done on the synthesized material's cytotoxic, antibacterial, bio-imaging, and anti-counterfeit properties.

The Boerhavia diffusa leaves were collected from areas in and around Kattankulathur, Chengalpet District, Tamil Nadu. Ethylenediamine (99 % purity) was procured from SRL Chemicals, India. Deionized (DI) water was used throughout the experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N-CDs from Boerhavia diffusa leaves extract
ce:section.ce:para: In this process, about 30 mL of the leaf extract was collected by grinding and 1 mL of ethylene diamine was added to the Teflon-lined steel autoclave and it was heated for about 4 h at 180 ̊C in a hot air oven. The autoclave was let to cool to ambient temperature following the procedure. Once it reaches room temperature, the material is filtered to remove the residue and the filtrate gives the bright green fluorescent N-CDs (as shown in Fig. 1 ) which were collected and stored @ 4 ̊C for additional characterization and research [38].

In order to evaluate the optical characteristics of the produced nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs), a SHIMADZU UV-3600 PLUS UV–visible spectrophotometer was used. Using CuKα radiation at 40 kV and a scan range of 20° to 80°, PANalytical X-ray diffraction (XRD) was used to assess the structural properties and amorphous nature of the N-CDs. A Japanese JEOL JEM-2100 Plus High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HR-TEM) was used to examine the interior surface morphology and selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns. The SHIMADZU IRTRACER 100 spectrometer was used to perform Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy in ATR mode (FTIR). Using tools from Physical Electronics, the produced N-CDs were subjected to X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS).

The agar well diffusion method was used to evaluate the synthetic N-CDs' antibacterial efficacy against gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria. For each test culture, a sterile swab soaked in a bacterial suspension containing 10⁶ CFU/ml was used to evenly spread the inoculum onto nutrient agar plates. After making 8 mm-diameter wells in the agar, the samples were left to diffuse for two hours at room temperature. The plates then underwent a 24-hour, upright incubation period at 37 °C. A common antibiotic used for comparison was ciproflaxin. Following the incubation period, millimetres were used to measure the widths of the inhibition zones surrounding the bacterial growth. By dispersing across the agar media, the antibacterial antibiotic successfully stopped the growth of the tested bacterial strains.

The MTT assay, as outlined by Mosmann (1983), was used in this investigation to assess the cytotoxic effects of the synthesized N-CDs on MDA MB 231 triple-negative breast cancer cells. The experiment used 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT), which is converted to formazan, a purple chemical, in live cells by mitochondrial succinate dehydrogenase and reductase. The quantity of viable cells has a direct correlation with formazan production, while the degree of cytotoxicity has an inverse relationship. 96-well plates containing trypsinized cells were incubated for 24 h at 37 °C. Following this first incubation, the MDA-MB-231 cells were exposed to different concentrations of the N-CD samples for 12 to 24 h before their vitality was evaluated.

The MDA-MB-231 cell line, which was cultivated in DMEM media and exposed to test chemicals for a full day, was used in a cell viability assay. To evaluate cell viability, MTT reagent was added after incubation. To facilitate cell imaging, about 1 × 104 MDA-MB-231 cells were seeded into culture plates and cultured for 24 h at 37 °C. Following the removal of the media, phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) was used to thoroughly wash the cells. Following the addition of 1 ml of PBS to the plates, the cells were examined using a fluorescence microscope.
In order to prevent counterfeiting, the synthesized N-CDs were used as writing ink and investigated for usage as luminous ink.

UV–visible absorption spectroscopy, as illustrated in Fig. 2 , is used to assess the optical characteristics of the produced N-CDs. The transitions from Π to Π* and n to Π* due to the C=C and C=O groups cause the absorption to peak between 250 and 380 nm [39]. The UV–vis spectrum of the Boerhavia diffusa leaves extract S 1a is given in the supplementary file.

Fig. 2 b) shows the Fluorescence emission spectra of the synthesized N-CDs at different excitation wavelengths and the results show that at excitation wavelengths 280 to 320 nm, the emission wavelength increases but after excitation wavelength 340 nm, the emission wavelength decreases. The maximum emission wavelength is found to be 355 nm when the excitation wavelength is 340 nm [40–42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characteristics of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: By analyzing their XRD pattern, the structural characteristics of the synthesized N-CDs were investigated. The large single peak in Fig. 3 a at 22.3° indicates that N-CDs are amorphous and that their graphitic nature is the cause of this broad peak, which is also very consistent with the literature [15,43]. The structural analysis of the Boerhavia diffusa leaves extract has been given in figure S 1b in the supplementary file.

As seen in Fig. 3b, FT-IR analysis was used to determine which functional groups were present in the synthesized N-CDs. Around 3400 cm−1, a stretching vibration was detected, which is consistent with –OH/–NH bonds. C–H bonds are responsible for the wide absorption peak at about 2825 cm−1. CO and CC stretching vibrations are represented by the peaks at 1747 cm−1 and 1643 cm−1, respectively. Furthermore, the broad peaks at around 1532 cm−1, 1388 cm−1, 1089 cm−1, and 1044 cm−1 are linked to stretching vibrations of C–N, C–OH, C–O–C, and C–H [44,45].

The elemental makeup of the produced N-CDs was examined using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The survey spectrum of the N-CDs is shown in Fig. 4 a which results in the three binding energies such that 531.5, 399.2, and 283.3 eV corresponds to the O 1s, N 1s, and C 1s spectra and its inset, where carbon (72 %), nitrogen (10 %), and oxygen (18 %) are present. The Carbon 1s spectrum is displayed in Fig. 4b. The peaks at 288.1, 285.9, and 284.8 eV correspond to the functional groups on the N-CDs that are CO/CN, C–OH/C–N, and C–C/CC. The nitrogen 1 s spectrum shows the peaks at 398, 399.5, and 400.5 eV correspond to the C–N–C, C–N–H, and (C3)–N, and the oxygen 1 s spectrum depicts the peak at 531 and 532.2 eV correspond to the C=O and C-O peaks which are shown in Fig. 4c and d, respectively. These functional groupings match those found in the FT-IR spectrum quite nicely [46–48].

ce:section.ce:section-title: TEM analysis and zeta potential of N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) was used to obtain the morphologies of the prepared N-CDs at various magnifications, as shown in Fig. 5 (a–c), the SAED pattern in Fig. 5(d), and the EDX analysis in Fig. 5(e). The N-CDs are spherical and less than 10 nm, according to the TEM pictures. The synthesized N-CDs' graphitic carbon structure is validated by the SAED pattern. The elemental composition of the N-CDs was also determined by EDX spectroscopy, which revealed the existence of carbon (C), nitrogen (N), as well as oxygen (O), and Fig. 5 e inlet shows the weight percentage of the N-CDs [45,49].

The particle size distribution of the N-CDs is shown in Fig. 6 a where its particle size is found to be 5.9 nm, The HRTEM image 5c showed that the graphitic layers have an interlayer spacing of 0.221 nm, exhibiting a narrow size distribution and consistent particle size. The zeta potential was performed to know the charge on the surface of the synthesized N-CDs in Fig. 6b, which shows a peak at −35.9 mV, revealing the N-CDs' stability.

The green synthesized N-CDs were studied as the anti-bacterial agent against the gram-positive E. coli and gram-negative S. aureus bacteria. The anti-bacterial activity was performed at different loadings of the N-CDs (25, 50, 75, 100) μl and Ciproflaxin was used as the standard to obtain the zone of inhibition. The results shown in Fig. 7 and Table 1 predict that at 100 μl concentration, the zone of inhibition is maximum i.e.15.51 mm and 16.84 mm for both gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria which is very much closer to the standard Ciproflaxin 24.27 mm and 25.50 mm for both the bacteria. It is observed that when the concentration is increased, the zone of inhibition can be increased to match the standard [47,50–52].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell cytotoxicity and bio-imaging of N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: N-CD’s cytotoxicity was evaluated against the MDA MB 231 human breast cancer cell line using the MTT Assay. The assessment was conducted at various N-CDs concentrations (512, 256, 128, 64, 32, 16, 8, 4, 2, 1) μl against the cancer cells for 24 h. At initial concentration, the N-CDs were non-toxic against the cell line and their IC50 value was found to be 108.62 μl. As the concentration increases the cell viability is decreased as shown in Fig. 8 and Table 2 . Upon modification of the concentration, it is suggested to be a promising candidate for bio-imaging application.

Here, we have analyzed N-CDs as bioimaging agents for which MDA MB 231 cell lines have been employed. The breast cancer cells were incubated and exposed to fluorescence microscopy, they exhibited bright green fluorescence, and the cancerous cells were spotted which is clearly seen in Fig. 9 . These findings confirm that the N-CDs are confined to the cell membrane and cytoplasm, preventing them from entering the nucleus [16,53,54]. This suggests that the prepared N-CDs serve as a promising bioimaging agent without affecting cell viability.

The fluorescence characteristics of the synthesized N-CDs were utilized to create fluorescent ink. Using this ink, the phrase “Department of Chemistry” along with our institution's name “SRMIST, KTR CAMPUS,” and two butterfly designs were written and drawn on a sheet of paper. As shown in Fig. 10 , the writing and drawings are invisible under normal light but become clearly visible when exposed to UV light. This demonstrates that our synthesized N-CDs have strong potential for anti-counterfeiting applications.

In this study, we successfully synthesized green, easy-to-produce, and biocompatible N-CDs from Boerhavia diffusa leaf extract, exhibiting excellent fluorescence properties. The structure and characteristics of the N-CDs were confirmed through HR-TEM, XRD, FT-IR, UV–visible spectroscopy, and XPS analysis. Following this confirmation, the synthesized N-CDs demonstrated strong antibacterial activity, showing a notable zone of inhibition. Their biocompatibility was further tested through cell viability studies, where they exhibited 97.66 % viability at lower concentrations, making them a promising tool for bio-imaging applications using MDA-MB231 human cancer cells. Additionally, the N-CDs were employed as an anti-counterfeiting agent. Overall, these green-synthesized N-CDs hold great potential as a sustainable and biocompatible nanoprobe.

Samira Khathoon Kalifathullah: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Conceptualization. Sangeetha Alaguvel: Writing – review & editing, Software, Data curation. Devikala Sundaramurthy: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: I thank NRC-SRMIST, and SCIF-SRMIST for giving us the good infrastructure to complete this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2025.114473.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Samira Khathoon Kalifathullah has joined in the year 2022 to pursue Ph.D. under the guidance of Dr. S. Devikala, Associate Professor, Department of Chemistry, SRM Institute of Science and Technology -Kattankulathur. She obtained her master’s degree from B.S. Abdur Rahman Crescent Institute of Science and Technology, Vandalur. Currently, she is working on Carbon Dots and their hybrid materials for Biological Applications.

Sangeetha Alaguvel has joined in the year 2022 to persue Ph.D. under the guidance of Dr. S. Devikala, Associate Professor, Department of Chemistry, SRM Institute of Science and Technology -Kattankulathur. She obtained her master’s degree from Sri Sarada College for Women, Salem. Currently, she is working on green-synthesized nanomaterials for Biological Applications.

Devikala Sundaramurthy obtained her PhD in 2013 from SRM Institute of Science and Technology, Kattankulathur, Tamil Nadu. Currently, she is working as an Associate Professor at Department of Chemistry SRM Institute of Science and Technology, Kattankulathur. Her research interests include Polymer Composites, Corrosion, Chemical Sensors, Organic synthesis, Dye-sensitized solar cells, and Biological Applications.
